content.plain_english: This week in Year 1 maths, your child is working on Time. The learning focuses on: tell the time to the hour and half past the hour; draw the hands on a clock face to show these times; and recognise and use language relating to dates, including:. These skills build directly on what they have already mastered and prepare them for the next steps in the programme of study.
content.in_school: Children use practical measuring equipment — rulers, scales, measuring cylinders, and clocks — to explore measurement in real contexts. They record findings using diagrams and number lines before progressing to written calculations. Problem-solving tasks show how these skills are used every day.
content.home_activity: Talk through the family routine using time language: 'We leave at quarter to 9', 'lunch is at half past 12'. Ask your child to read the time on an analogue clock whenever they pass one. Time simple activities — how long does it take to brush your teeth?
dinner_table_questions: Can you show me how to tell the time to the hour and half past the hour?
Was it easy or tricky to draw the hands on a clock face to show these times? Why?
How would you explain today's maths to someone who has never seen it before?

key_vocabulary.definition: A small unit of time — 60 seconds = 1 minute.
A unit of time — 60 minutes = 1 hour.
A unit of time — 24 hours = 1 day.
A clock with hands that show the time on a circular face.
How long something takes from start to finish.

content.times_table_tip: Year 1 focuses on the 2, 5, and 10 times tables. Try counting in 2s together on the stairs, or in 10s while counting out coins. Even 2–3 minutes a day builds the familiarity that pays off in Year 2.
